name: Decimals — Hundredths, the Second Seat
whatIsIt: Cut a tenth into ten tinier pieces and you get HUNDREDTHS — a whole cut into 100 equal parts. The dot's SECOND seat counts them: 0.25 = 25/100 (25 hundredths). You've been using hundredths all along — paise ARE hundredths of a rupee, and centimetres are hundredths of a metre!
whyNeeded: Tenths are too chunky for precise things: heights (1.37 m), race timings (10.85 seconds), test percentages, cricket averages! Hundredths give measurement its fine grain — and they unlock comparing decimals of different lengths without falling for tricks.
realLifeUses: Height: 1.37 metres = 1 m and 37 hundredths (37 cm!)
Money: ₹4.05 = 4 rupees and 5 paise (hundredths of a rupee)
Sports: a 100 m race won by 10.85 vs 10.9 seconds

vocabulary.meaning: One piece of a whole cut into 100 equal parts: 1/100 = 0.01.
The SECOND seat right of the dot: in 0.25, the 5 counts hundredths.
0.5 = 0.50 — same amount! (5 tenths = 50 hundredths — equivalent fractions in disguise!)

story.title: Squares of the Hundred-Bar
story.text: The halwai's big chikki is scored into 100 tiny squares. Ravi holds 25 squares, Anu holds 3. 'Write each as a decimal,' says the halwai. 'Ravi has 25 hundredths — 25/100 = 0.25. Anu has 3 hundredths — 3/100 = 0.03. See how the zero guards the tenths seat so the 3 stays in the hundredths place!'
story.extractedProblem: Ravi holds 25 squares of a 100-square bar; Anu holds 3. Write each as a decimal.
story.answerInStory: Ravi: 25/100 = 0.25. Anu: 3/100 = 0.03 — the zero guards the tenths seat!

visual.caption: Each tenth splits into 10 hundredths: 0.25 = 2 tenths + 5 hundredths = 25/100. And 0.5 = 0.50 — same amount!

teachingGallery.title: Hundredths — the second place after the dot
teachingGallery.note: Cut a tenth into 10 again: 0.01. Two digits after the dot = hundredths.

examples.caption: 0.99 is just one hundredth short of 1.

standardMethod.summary: Two seats after the dot: tenths then hundredths. Read the pair as 'hundredths'.
steps: First seat = tenths, second seat = hundredths.
Zeros guard seats: 0.03 = 3 hundredths (no tenths!); 0.30 = 0.3 (trailing zero adds nothing).
Compare seat by seat from the left: 0.4 vs 0.35 → tenths 4 > 3 → 0.4 wins!

alternateMethods.name: Money translation
alternateMethods.whenToUse: Whenever hundredths feel abstract — coins make them real!
steps: Think of the number as rupees: hundredths = paise.
Which is more, 0.3 or 0.25? → 30 paise vs 25 paise. Done!
alternateMethods.example: 1.37 m = ₹1.37 imagined as money → 1 rupee 37 paise — the pattern is identical.

alternateMethods.name: Hundred-grid picture
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To SEE hundredths and equivalent decimals.
steps: Shade squares: each = 0.01; each full row = 0.1.
0.30 = 3 full rows = 0.3 — the grid PROVES they're equal!
The tenths seat needs its zero guard.

workedExamples.problem: Race timings: Anu 10.9 seconds, Ravi 10.85 seconds. Ravi cheers 'I took LONGER — 85 beats 9!' Who was actually faster?
steps: Compare seat by seat: whole seconds tie (10).
Tenths: Anu 9, Ravi 8 → Ravi's time is SMALLER (10.85 < 10.90).
Smaller time = faster runner — Ravi WON but read it backwards! Longer decimals aren't bigger.

trickPractice.intro: Out of a hundred? Fill exactly two spots after the dot — pad with a zero if you must.

hintLadder: Hundredths fill TWO places after the dot.
Write the number in those two places (add a leading 0 if needed).
Hundredths fill TWO places after the dot.
Write the number in those two places (add a leading 0 if needed).
Hundredths fill TWO places after the dot.
Write the number in those two places (add a leading 0 if needed).
Hundredths fill TWO places after the dot.
Write the number in those two places (add a leading 0 if needed).
Hundredths fill TWO places after the dot.
Write the number in those two places (add a leading 0 if needed).
Hundredths fill TWO places after the dot.
Write the number in those two places (add a leading 0 if needed).

commonMistakes.fix: The zero GUARDS the tenths seat! 3/100 has no tenths → 0.03. Money check: 3 paise is ₹0.03, never ₹0.30.
Compare seat by seat from the left! Tenths first: 4 beats 3, so 0.4 > 0.35. Or money: 40 paise beats 35 paise.
5 tenths = 50 hundredths — equivalent fractions in decimal clothes! A trailing zero after the last decimal digit changes NOTHING: 0.5 = 0.50. (The grid shows 5 rows either way!)

easy.q: A 100-square chocolate bar: you take 25 squares. Write your share as a decimal.
hintLadder: Zero wholes, then 25 in the two seats.
Are there any TENTHS in 3 hundredths?
No — so the tenths seat needs its guard.

challenge.q: Your height is 1 metre and 37 centimetres. A centimetre is a hundredth of a metre — write your height in metres as a decimal.
hintLadder: 1 whole metre left of the dot.
37 hundredths of a metre after it.

challenge.q: Order these race times, FASTEST first: 10.9, 10.85, 11.02

options.label: 0.09 — nine hundredths is less than ten hundredths

questions.answer: 0.09 — nine hundredths is less than ten hundredths

questions.q: ₹5 and 8 paise, written as a decimal amount:

teachBackPrompt: Teach the second seat! Draw a 10×10 grid for someone at home, shade 0.25 and 0.03, and explain why the zero in 0.03 can never be skipped — use paise if it helps!

revisionCard.summary: Second seat = hundredths: 0.25 = 25/100. Zero guards seats (3/100 = 0.03, NOT 0.3!). Trailing zeros change nothing (0.5 = 0.50). Compare seat by seat from the left (0.4 > 0.35). Money = built-in hundredths practice!

gameMission.title: The Hundred-Crumb Heist
gameMission.brief: The legendary 100-square chocolate is being shared — but decimal labels keep getting scrambled! Write each share correctly, spot the guard-zero fakes (0.3 vs 0.03!), and rank the race-time trophies.

realLifeProject: Measure your family: heights in metres as decimals (1.37 m!), using a tape. Then check a shop receipt for paise amounts — find one price whose hundredths seat is doing real work.

rememberIt.hook: Second place after the dot = hundredths (1/100)
rememberIt.unpack: 0.07 = 7 hundredths = 7/100. 0.25 = 25/100 = 1/4. Two decimal places means hundredths.

studentQuestions.q: What does the second decimal place mean?
studentQuestions.a: Hundredths - one of 100 equal pieces. 0.03 is 3 hundredths, 3/100.
